steps.title: Distinguish random selection from random assignment
steps.content:
Random assignment to treatment groups allows us to infer causation (does watering pattern cause growth differences). Random selection from a population is what determines which population we can generalize to.

This study used both: the trees were randomly selected from a habitat, then randomly assigned to treatment groups.

Generalizability depends on the selection, not the assignment.

steps.title: Identify the source population
steps.content:
The 50 trees were randomly selected from all 17-year-old baobab trees in this habitat.

This is the population they represent. Because the selection was random, we can generalize the findings about how watering patterns affect growth from the 50-tree sample to the entire population from which they were drawn.

steps.title: Check constraints: age and location
steps.content:
Two boundaries limit the scope:
  • Age: The study began with 17-year-old trees. We learned how 17-year-olds respond to watering; we don't know if 19-year-olds would respond the same way. (Choice B is out.)
  • Location: The sample came from one habitat, not all of South Africa. Different habitats have different soil, climate, and water availability, which could change the treatment effect. (Choice C is out.)
Choice A just restates the sample, not the population we generalize to.

steps.title: Identify the largest applicable population
steps.content:
Combining the principle (random selection from a population) with the constraints (age 17, location this habitat), the results can be applied to all baobab trees that were 17 years old in this habitat — the full population from which the random sample was drawn.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
